steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says Patel's team searched for "Twitter" on academic databases and that a few results discussed "sounds made by tractor engines" rather than the social media site. The blank must supply a logical explanation for why papers about tractor-engine sounds would appear in a search for the word "Twitter." The explanation must link the search term to those off-topic papers.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘a group of researchers had an extensive discussion on Twitter about the design of tractor engines.’: This says the conversation happened on the platform, not in academic papers. It would not explain why database records about tractor sounds contain the word "Twitter." ✗
  • Choice B — ‘most of the papers in the Scopus and Web of Science databases do not discuss automotive engines or social media websites.’: This is a claim about database content in general and explains nothing about how tractor-engine papers appeared in this specific search. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘the word "twitter" may not always refer to a social media website, but to other subjects such as noises made by machines.’: The word "twitter" also means a light, chirping sound — exactly the kind of sound a tractor engine can make. Papers using the word in that sense would be indexed and returned by a keyword search for "Twitter," explaining the anomalous results. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘academics who are active on social networks are likely to announce their recent publications on Twitter.’: This is about authors' social media behavior, not about why papers on engine sounds appear in the search results. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
